title: Volatility sizer cannot use its ATR fallback stop

evidence.quote: The function returns zero when StopLoss is zero, then later checks if StopLoss is zero to set atrStopLoss.
CanonicalSizer chooses volatility sizing when ATR and Volatility are set, but only returns it if the size is non-zero.
The test named uses volatility-based sizer omits StopLoss but only asserts the final size is greater than zero.

reasoning: The zero StopLoss guard makes the ATR-derived stop-loss branch unreachable. A config with ATR and Volatility but no explicit StopLoss silently falls through CanonicalSizer to another sizing method, despite the test name and code path implying volatility sizing should apply.
reproduction: NewVolatilityBasedSizer(2).CalculateSize(PositionSizingConfig{Capital:10000, CurrentPrice:100, ATR:2, Volatility:0.02}) returns zero instead of sizing from an ATR-based stop near 96.
recommendation: Remove StopLoss from the early return, require CurrentPrice/ATR/Volatility as appropriate, compute atrStopLoss before validating riskPerShare, and then use explicit StopLoss only when provided.
whyTestsDoNotAlreadyCoverThis: VolatilityBasedSizer tests always provide StopLoss for positive cases. CanonicalSizer's volatility test only checks size > 0, so the fixed-fractional fallback satisfies it.
suggestedRegressionTest: Assert the direct volatility sizer returns the expected ATR-derived size when StopLoss is zero, and assert CanonicalSizer returns that same size when ATR and Volatility are set.
